topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a bright, insistent wave washing over the crowded ballroom. I leaned against a marble pillar, the cool stone a momentary anchor against the swirling chaos. My eyes scanned the room, cataloging faces I vaguely recognized, a dull ache settling behind my ribs. Then, there he was. Standing near the cake table, laughing with someone whose arm was draped casually around his waist. It was him. The air seemed to thin out immediately, the vibrant colors of the decorations blurring into a muted wash. I felt a sudden, sharp tightening in my chest, a familiar, unwelcome pressure. I forced myself to take a slow sip of my lukewarm champagne, the bubbles doing little to settle the restlessness churning inside me. He caught my gaze for a fraction of a second, and a flicker—something unreadable, maybe recognition, maybe just acknowledgment—passed between us before he turned back to the celebration. I pushed off the pillar, needing distance more than I needed to stay.

[story 2]

I kept my head down, focusing intently on the intricate patterns of the carpet beneath my feet. Every forced smile felt like a physical strain, pulling at the muscles around my jaw. The noise of the celebration was a relentless assault, each burst of laughter grating on my nerves. I was trying to be invisible, a ghost navigating a sea of well-dressed acquaintances. Then, the periphery of my vision snagged on him. He was across the room, talking animatedly, his posture relaxed, completely at ease. A sudden, unwelcome drop in my stomach made my hands instinctively clench into fists at my sides. It was an instant, jarring realignment of my entire internal state. I wanted nothing more than to melt into the shadows, to simply cease existing in this loud, glittering space. I took a deep, shallow breath, the scent of expensive perfume momentarily overwhelming me, and resolved to simply endure the next hour.

[story 3]

The sheer volume of the party was overwhelming; it pressed in on me from all sides, a suffocating blanket of forced gaiety. I moved through the throng with practiced, minimal engagement, my gaze darting everywhere but directly at the source of the most jarring disruption. And then, there he was, standing near the entrance, looking entirely too bright amidst the softer lighting. Seeing him sent a strange, heavy slump through my shoulders. My thoughts slowed, becoming thick and sluggish, like honey poured too slowly. I watched him for a beat too long, cataloging the subtle shifts in his expression, a strange, hollow echo resonating in the quiet space behind my eyes. A profound sense of exhaustion washed over me, a deep-seated need for quiet solitude that the music and the crowd refused to grant. I decided, with a sigh that escaped without conscious effort, that I needed to find the exit.
